Let's discuss your deal criteria on your first deals. My advice is print this A to Z list and keep it somewhere very visible in your office, bulletin board, fridge, wherever. This is the deal criteria checklist which especially for your first deal you'll be able to refer back to and check off and sure this is your great first deal. As you're searching or working with a broker which we'll touch on in this lesson make sure every deal fits this complete criteria and that you personally tackle all the steps outlined. Okay, A, A is the size of the property. Remember 16 units or more, 13 is ideal. Bigger than that is even better than ideal. B, you love the property location. When you first visited you felt good. You loved it. When you drove back a second time you loved it. When you drove back a third time and started looking inside and outside and behind you loved the property. C, it's cash flow positive without any improvements, without a rehab and you're collecting 4-6% or more on the cash you invested. D, you use debt and no more than 75% as low as 65% and you love this deal even with the debt load. E, have an active manager on the property, a good manager that you can pay a good rate to or a little more that you can provide great management on the property and you feel good about them being there to take care of the tenants. F, you have a 1.25% DCR or higher. This is part of your checklist. G, you've set aside $250 to $300 per unit or more for future renovations before you calculated your cash flow. H, you have an actual insurance quote on the property before moving forward. I, assume increases in property taxes at full rates and if you need to adjust backwards from that a little bit, no less than 85% of the full millage rate on property taxes. J, use no less than 40% for your expense calculation or more to still calculate your cash flow. Assume no increase in the effective collected income of the property. L, you have all obvious repair costs for the next 3 years in reserve. Set aside. M, you know your break-even occupancy, a.k.a. you've done worst case scenarios and know this somewhere under 90%, 82 even, lower in the lower 80, know your break-even occupancy, a.k.a. your worst case scenario. N, know your financial statement inside and out and know it without having to refer to it. O, use a broker on the deal. If you need to do a deal without a broker, this is not a deal, particularly your first deal. Walk and create folders on at least 25 other deals before you commit to this one. Q, produce $24,000 per year regardless of your cash on cash percentage. So in addition to the requirement to have a 4-6% cash on cash, make sure your property produces at least $24,000 in positive cash flow. R, you have a minimum $25,50 and $75 rent increase potential over the next 1-year, 2-year and 3-years. That means there's a potential on this property in the future to raise the rents 25, do a calculation on what 50 does, and then 75 if you got that on 90% of the units that you're purchasing. S, have a solid exit plan for the property including price, cap rate, even the type of buyer. T, do an 8% exit calculation. That is the price that you can sell at to the new buyer, 3, 5, 7 years in the future that will provide that buyer with an 8% cash on cash return. You heard me right. You bought the deal, assuming a 4-6, but you want to be able to sell at a price that they can earn 8%. That means you have a desirable product to sell into the marketplace and still return the next investor a hefty cash on cash return. You have loan underwriting done by a third party without the influence of how you underwrite the deal or your opinion of the deal. That means I want a third party, the bank, a broker on the finance side to underwrite your deal without your influence. V, use worst case scenarios on income and expenses and work that scenario out on every model possible using your four quadrants. W, know the cost per door of expenses on the properties in your market excluding taxes. There's a metric in every market that will tell you exactly what the per door cost average median is on expenses for apartments in your desirable market. X, underwrite your deal, assuming all debt, including all promises to partners, including a salary to the manager even if it's you and all principal and interest when calculating your cash flow. Y, assume two years of break even even though you know there won't be break even in the first two years. And then assume no cash flow and decide do you still love the property even though none of that might be true. And Z, all done by the deal. You found a deal, go forward and if it's big enough and you can't do it by yourself, call me a card on capital, I might do that deal with you. So here's your A to Z list. Use it on every deal. Make this your Bible checklist for determining whether or not you've checked off every box. Whether this is your right first deal, on that first deal you need help, you need a partner, you need some brains and confidence with you so that you can pull the trigger and either go raise money from investors, borrow money from mom and dad, take on debt. Let us help you if we can. Use your A to Z checklist because when you call me, I'm going to say, dude show me your checklist.
